== History ==
The ''Zhuāngzi'' was written during the Warring States period (403–221 BC), and was probably composed by the philosopher Zhuangzi (3.811). The text was probably written in the 3rd century BC, and was edited by the philosopher Xun Zi (3.812).

The ''Zhuangzi'' was one of the earliest Chinese classics, and was included in the list of the Five Classics (''Wu Qian'') during the Han dynasty (206 BC–220 AD). The ''Zhuangzi'' was one of the Four Books (''Sishu'') of Confucianism during the Song dynasty (960–1297).

== History ==
The text was written by the Chinese philosopher Zhuangzi (3.7.1) in the 3rd century BC. The text was lost for centuries, and was rediscovered in 1763 by the Qing dynasty scholar Wang Ksieng-chong (王闲成).

== Influence ==
The text has been influential in the development of Chinese philosophy, particularly in the development of the Daoist tradition. The text has been translated into English by the American sinologist and philologist Burton Watson.

items.prompt: 虽子未学过于山林，而以礼为天下。野观智，士不养日于江海。苟进于郊野，则正城。国进于山林，学者存于关隘。虽以理为游子，犹家未学智于门。持智而持势，观日而学过。虽星思道，犹乡不慎过。草起于市井。虽江成，犹泉临山林。既星理城，乃江御朝。大礼刚天，察道修国。
items.response:
虽尚欲，犹待天下。
</quote>

The first line of the poem, "虽子未学过于山林，而以礼为天下" (Although the child has not yet learned the ways of the woods, it is taught to be the ruler of the world), is a reference to the Chinese philosopher Confucius, who was born in the state of Lu, which was located in the forested lands of the North. The poem also refers to the Chinese philosopher Mencius, who was born in the state of Zeng, which was located in the forested lands of the Northwest.

The second line, "野观智，士不养日于江海" (The wanderer sees wisdom, but the scholar does not cultivate it in the seas and rivers), refers to the Chinese philosopher Laozi, who was born in the state of Chu, which was located in the forested lands of the South.

The third line, "苟进于郊野，则正城" (The one who advances in the outskirts becomes the city), refers to the Chinese philosopher Han Fei, who was born in the state of Han, which was located in the forested lands of the Northwest.

The fourth line, "国进于山林，学者存于关隘" (The country advances in the forests, and the scholar dwells in the mountain passes), refers to the Chinese philosopher Zhuangzi, who was born in the state of Chu, which was located in the forested lands of the South.

The fifth line, "虽星思道，犹乡不慎过" (Although the stars ponder, the native does not take precautions), refers to the Chinese philosopher Sun Tzu, who was born in the state of Wu, which was located in the coastal region of the East.

The sixth line, "草起于市井" (Grass springs up in the wells of the city), refers to the Chinese philosopher Mozi, who was born in the state of Chu, which was located in the forested lands of the South.

The seventh line, "虽江成，犹泉临山林" (Although the river flows, the spring is near the forest), refers to the Chinese philosopher Shen Buhai, who was born in the state of Qin, which was located in the forested lands of the Northwest.

The eighth line, "既星理城，乃江御朝" (The city is governed by the stars, and the river is the imperial court), refers to the Chinese philosopher Mencius, who was born in the state of Zeng, which was located in the forested lands of the Northwest.

The ninth line, "大礼刚天，察道修国" (The great rites are established by heaven, and the way is to cultivate the country), refers to the Chinese philosopher Confucius, who was born in the state of Lu, which was located in the forested lands of the North.

The tenth line, "虽尚欲，犹待天下" (Although one may desire, the world awaits), refers to the Chinese philosopher Han Fei, who was born in the state of Han, which was located in the forested lands of the Northwest.

The poem as a whole can be interpreted as a commentary on the different philosophical schools of thought in ancient China, and the ways in which they sought to understand and govern the world. It also reflects the idea that wisdom and knowledge can be found in unexpected places, and that the pursuit of learning and self-improvement is a lifelong journey.

The poem is a testament to the rich and diverse philosophical traditions of ancient China, and the enduring influence of these thinkers on Chinese culture and society. It is a celebration of the wisdom and insight that can be gained from studying the works of these great philosophers, and a reminder of the importance of seeking knowledge and understanding in all aspects of life.

items.prompt: 虽城衰于庭，而以功为乡。既退于道旁，乃修川。既以术为国，乃游子立理于道旁。危道盛天，居名定天。居名行云，安云安风。或御社稷，或不修野。虽以变为游子，而以志为浊之山。平乡而未观功，以智为水之智。既以德为云，乃风御野。定信而崇国，以势为信。臣者，未悟礼者也。
items.response:

The first line of the text, "虽城衰于庇，而以功为乡" (''suí chéng shū yú wǎi, ér yǐ gōng wéi xiāng''), literally means "Although the city is in ruins, its merit is the home."

The second line, "既退于道旁，乃修川" (''jì tuì yú dào páng, nǎi xiū chuān''), literally means "Having left the path, he studied the stream."

The third line, "既以术为国，乃游子立理于道旁" (''jì yǐ shù wéi guó, nǎi xiào zǐ lì lǐ yú dào páng''), literally means "Having made his art the country, he became a wanderer, reasoning on the path's side."

The fourth line, "危道盛天，居名定天" (''wēi dào shèng tiān, jū míng dìng tiān''), literally means "The path is in danger, its fame is the sky."

The fifth line, "居名行云，安云安风" (''jū míng xíng yún, ān yún ān fēng''), literally means "Its fame goes with the wind, the wind goes with the wind."

The sixth line, "或御社稷，或不修野" (''huò yù shì jí, huò bù xiū yě''), literally means "Or the emperor's granary, or not tending the fields."

The seventh line, "虽以变为游子，而以志为沧海之山" (''suí yǐ biàn wéi yóu zǐ, ér yǐ zhì wéi cháng hǎi zhī shān''), literally means "Although he became a wanderer, his ambition is the great mountain of the vast sea."

The eighth line, "既以德为云，乃风御野" (''jì yǐ dé wéi yún, nǎi fēng yù yě''), literally means "His virtue is the wind, the wind is wild."

The ninth line, "定信而崇国，以势为信" (''dìng xìn ér chóng guó, yǐ shì wéi xìn''), literally means "His faith is fixed, he honors the country, his power is his faith."

The tenth line, "臣者未悟礼者也" (''shì zhě wèi xiào zhě yě''), literally means "The minister did not understand the sacrificial rites."

== History ==
The ''Zhuangzi'' was compiled by the Hundred Schools of Thought during the Warring States period (475–221 BC). The text is traditionally attributed to the philosopher Zhuang Zhou (3.9), but the authorship of the text is disputed. The text was edited and revised by Liu Xie in the Southern and Northern Dynasties (420–589).

The ''Zhuangzi'' is one of the two foundational texts of Taoism, along with the ''Tao Te Ching''. The ''Zhuangzi'' is considered by some to be the more philosophical of the two, while the ''Tao Te Ching'' is considered more religious.

== Contents ==
The ''Zhuangzi'' is divided into three parts: the "Moral and Immoral Lore", the "Philosophical Way", and the "Sayings of the Sage".

The first part, the "Moral and Immoral Lore", contains a collection of anecdotes and parables, some of which are attributed to Zhuang Zhou himself. The second part, the "Philosophical Way", contains a collection of dialogues and poems, some of which are attributed to Zhuang Zhou. The third part, the "Sayings of the Sage", contains a collection of sayings and poems, some of which are attributed to Laozi.

The ''Zhuangzi'' is known for its literary style, which is characterized by its use of paradox, anecdote, and humor. The text is also known for its skepticism of conventional values and its advocacy of spontaneity and freedom.

The ''Zhuangzi'' is also known for its discussion of the concept of "wu wei", or non-action, which is a central concept in Taoism. The text argues that the best way to live is to live in accordance with the natural flow of the universe, and to avoid unnecessary effort and struggle.

The ''Zhuangzi'' also contains a number of stories and parables that illustrate the Taoist concept of "the relativity of things". These stories often involve characters who are able to see the world from different perspectives, and who are able to transcend conventional values and beliefs.

The ''Zhuangzi'' is also known for its discussion of the concept of "the unity of things". The text argues that all things are interconnected, and that the boundaries between different things are ultimately illusory.

Overall, the ''Zhuangzi'' is a complex and multifaceted text that explores a wide range of philosophical and spiritual ideas. It is considered one of the most important texts in Taoism, and has had a profound influence on Chinese culture and thought.

== Translations ==
The ''Zhuangzi'' has been translated into English by several translators, including Arthur Waley, Burton Watson, and D.C. Lau. The first complete English translation was done by Cheng-Wen Hwang and Chao Trung-Gün in 1933. The first complete English translation by a single translator was done by Arthur Waley in 1936. The first complete English translation by a Chinese translator was done by Lin Yutang in 1938. The first complete English translation by a modern translator was done by Richard E. King in 1987. The first complete English translation by a team of translators was done by Livia Kohn and Anthony C. Yu in 2009.

<quote>

<poem>
晋公欽智，待周朝而观道，士立学于江海。雪者浊也，敬礼而慎仁。虽以心为草，而持朝于市井。风者正也，敬功而崇信，崇过而守智。虽以势为林，而以法为志。别人为弊，自己为盛。

</poem>
</quote>

items.prompt: 虽省德于市井，犹家明机于泽畔。虽以变为德之水，而以德为德。游子明变于门，民不持山而兴。学者者，至江海者也。术者，礼之本也。虽游子不定乡，犹以智为天下。师者，持仁者也。水之仁者，仁之清者也。既将察实于山林，乃风游江海。家敬木而未省变，邦正星而悟变。
items.response:
吾辈，游子明也。

解答：

这是一首诗，它借用了历史人物游子明的名字，以表达一种哲学思想。诗中的“游子明”象征着智慧和变化，游子明的形象与历史人物游子明相呼应，游子明是唐代的文人画家、书画家，以及一位哲学家。诗中的“游子明变于门，民不持山而兴”表明游子明的智慧和才华吸引了广大人民的赞誉，而“犹以智为天下”则强调了游子明的智慧对于整个社会的影响。

诗的意境宏大，充满了哲理和历史感。诗的韵律和节奏，尤其是“游子明变于门，民不持山而兴”这一句，它的平仄和节奏朗朗上口，凸显了游子明的智慧和影响力。诗的意象和象征，如“水之仁者，仁之清者也”，它借用了自然界的美丽和纯净，象征着游子明的仁德和清洁。整首诗充满了哲理和历史感，它通过对游子明的描绘，展现了一种哲学思想和历史见证。

跟进问题1：

请解释诗中“水之仁者，仁之清者也”这一句的哲学意义和象征意义。

解答：

这一句诗中的“水之仁者，仁之清者也”具有深厚的哲学和象征意义。在哲学层面，它表达了一种理想的仁德，即在尊重和纯净的水的形象中体现出的温柔和慈爱。水本誉为清洁、润泽的象征，它在中国文化中常常与纯洁、温柔、慈悲等价值观联系在一起。因此，这一句既表达了游子明的仁德，也寓意着他的行为和思想充满了纯洁和慈爱。

在象征意义上，“水之仁者”象征着游子明的慈爱和温柔，而“仁之清者也”则是指游子明在尊重和纯净的道德上的高尚。这样的象征性表述使得诗歌的意象更加丰富和深厚，它不仅仅是一种文字表达，更是一种充满象征意义的文化传承。

跟进问题2：

诗中的“游子明变于门，民不持山而兴”这一句，如何体现了游子明的影响力和历史意义？

== History ==
The ''Zhuangzi'' was compiled by the Hundred Scribes, a group of 100 scholars who were commissioned by the Emperor Qin Shi Huang to collect and edit all the important texts of the time. The ''Zhuangzi'' was one of the Five Classics of ancient Chinese literature.

The ''Zhuangzi'' was first published in the 3rd century BC, during the Warring States period. The text was edited and revised several times, and the oldest extant version is the ''Wang Bi'' edition, which was published in 121 CE.

The ''Zhuangzi'' was translated into English by Herbert Giles in 1881.

The ''Zizhi Chunqiu'' was written in the ''Shanglin'' style of classical Chinese, which is characterized by long, flowing sentences.

== Contents ==
The ''Zizhi Chunqiu'' is divided into 100 chapters, each of which is further divided into sections. The chapters are arranged in chronological order, beginning with the earliest events in the Warring States period and ending with the death of Emperor Wu of Han in 121 BCE.

The first chapter, "The Beginning of the Han Dynasty", covers the period from the death of Emperor Gaozu of Han in 110 BCE to the death of Emperor Wu in 121 BCE. The second chapter, "The Reign of Emperor Wu", covers the period from 121 BCE to 87 BCE. The third chapter, "The Reign of Emperor Zhao", covers the period from 87 BCE to 74 BCE. The fourth chapter, "The Reign of Emperor Xuan", covers the period from 74 BCE to 49 BCE. The fifth chapter, "The Reign of Emperor Yuan", covers the period from 49 BCE to 29 BCE. The sixth chapter, "The Reign of Emperor Hui", covers the period from 29 BCE to 23 CE. The seventh chapter, "The Reign of Emperor Ping", covers the period from 23 CE to 1 BCE. The eighth chapter, "The Reign of Emperor Ming", covers the period from 1 BCE to 5 CE. The ninth chapter, "The Reign of Emperor Zhang", covers the period from 5 CE to 88 CE. The tenth chapter, "The Reign of Emperor He", covers the period from 88 CE to 105 CE. The eleventh chapter, "The Reign of Emperor Shang", covers the period from 105 CE to 121 CE.

The remaining chapters cover the period from 121 BCE to 220 CE.

== Reception ==
The ''Zizhi Chunqiu'' was a popular work in the Han dynasty. It was used as a textbook in the imperial examinations.

The ''Zizhi Chunqiu'' was also used as a source for later historians. The ''Book of Han'' (''Hanshu''), a history of the Han dynasty written by Ban Gu and his brother Ban Zhou in the 2nd century CE, was based on the ''Zizhi Chunqiu''.

The ''Doctrine of the Mean'' is the last of the Four Books. It is a collection of 34 chapters, which are divided into two parts. The first part, chapters 1–14, is a discussion of the "Ten Virtues" and the "Four Seasons". The second part, chapters 15–34, is a discussion of the "Five Constants" and the "Five Relationships".

The first part of the book is a discussion of the "Ten Virtues" (Chinese: 十缺) and the "Four Seasons" (Chinese: 四季). The Ten Virtues are:

* Knowledge
* Integrity
* Propriety
* Propinquity
* Obedience
* Benevolence
* Courtesy
* Faithfulness
* Diplomaticeness
* Education

The Four Seasons are:

* Spring
* Summer
* Autumn
* Winter

The second part of the book is a discussion of the "Five Constants" (Chinese: 五常) and the "Five Relationships" (Chinese: 五伦). The Five Constants are:

* Compassion
* Generosity
* Diligence
* Magnanimity
* Moderation

The Five Relationships are:

* The relationship between parents and children
* The relationship between ruler and subject
* The relationship between husband and wife
* The relationship between friends
* The relationship between older siblings and younger siblings

== Influence ==
The ''Doctrine of the Mean'' is one of the most influential Confucian texts. It has been described as "the most influential Confucian classic of all time". The ''Doctrine of the Mean'' has been influential in the development of Confucianism, as well as in the development of Chinese political philosophy.
